stem: What makes a password hardest to crack?

choices.text: Replacing letters with digits, as in P@ssw0rd
Being forced to change it every month
Making it long, unpredictable and unique to every site
Adding an exclamation mark at the end

explanation: Strength comes above all from length and unpredictability, and uniqueness keeps a single leak from compromising all of your accounts.

stem: An email from your “bank” urges you to click a link to “avoid having your account suspended”. What is the best reflex?

choices.text: Do not click the link and go to the bank's official site yourself
Click quickly, before the account gets suspended
Reply to the email to ask for confirmation
Forward the email to your contacts to warn them

explanation: Going through the official site you type in yourself neutralizes the booby-trapped link, whereas clicking or replying plays right into the attacker's hands.

stem: Why does two-factor authentication protect you even if your password leaks?

choices.text: It makes the password impossible to guess
It encrypts your Internet connection
It replaces the password with a fingerprint
The attacker must also supply a second factor that they do not have

explanation: The second factor (phone, key, temporary code) is exactly what the attacker lacks, which stops them from signing in despite the stolen password.

stem: Why should security updates be installed quickly?

choices.text: To get new interface colors
Because they fix known flaws that attackers are actively exploiting
Because they always make the device faster
Because otherwise the hardware warranty is voided

explanation: A patch makes the flaw public, and devices that have not been updated become easy targets for an exploit that is already available.

stem: What does the 3-2-1 backup rule say?

choices.text: Use 3 passwords, 2 devices and 1 antivirus
Back up 3 times a day onto 2 USB sticks
Three copies of the data, on two types of media, one of them kept off-site
Keep the data for 3 years on 2 drives stored in 1 drawer

explanation: The rule aims at redundancy and dispersion: three copies, two distinct types of media and one off-site copy to survive a local disaster.

stem: On public Wi-Fi, which risk is still very real today?

choices.text: A fake access point (evil twin) that imitates the venue's network to trap you
HTTPS stops encrypting your data as soon as Wi-Fi is involved
Anyone can read your banking password, even on an HTTPS site
Public Wi-Fi automatically deletes your files

explanation: HTTPS protects the content you exchange, but a fake access point remains a concrete threat because it controls the network you connect to.

stem: What does the HTTPS padlock really guarantee?

choices.text: That the site is honest and trustworthy
That the site contains no viruses
That the information the site displays is true
That the connection is encrypted and that you really are talking to the domain shown

explanation: HTTPS provides transport encryption and domain identity, but says nothing about how honest or reputable the owner is.

stem: Why limit the permissions granted to mobile apps?

choices.text: So the battery lasts exactly twice as long
To apply least privilege: an app only reaches what it strictly needs, which reduces the risk in case of abuse or a flaw
Because permissions slow down the Wi-Fi
Because otherwise the screen no longer locks

explanation: Restricting access applies least privilege and limits the data exposed if the app turns out to be malicious or compromised.
